target.name: Q-Factor
target.founder: Nir Davidson, Ofer Firstenberg, Yoav Sagi, Guy Raz

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $24M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing

data.narrative:
Q-Factor is a Seed-stage quantum computing company that has raised $24M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a universe of 11 quantum computing peers—3 public (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

The Stage-Progression method is applicable and yields a $60M valuation. This approach uses the $24M total raised as a proxy for current enterprise value and applies a 2.5x step-up multiplier typical of Seed-to-next-stage progression in venture. The Peer-Median method is not applicable; while 7 same-sector peers exist, insufficient clustering emerges within narrow filters (same-stage, adjacent-stage, similar-tier valuation brackets)—fewer than the three datapoints required for meaningful median calibration. A broad-sector median would risk material distortion given the heterogeneity of business models and maturity across the quantum computing landscape.

The triangulated valuation range is $51M to $69M, with a median of $60M. This range reflects a 15% downside and 15% upside variance around the stage-progression point estimate. Key uncertainties include the absence of direct Seed-stage peer benchmarking and Q-Factor's specific technology roadmap relative to the broader competitive set. The single-method result argues for caution in narrow range confidence; validation against additional data (revenue trajectory, IP portfolio strength, customer traction) would strengthen conviction.
